title: Tracking core inflation revisions
code: library(fred)

title: Tracking core inflation revisions
text: simple comparison analysts start : headline CPI vs core CPI (food energy excluded), see today. shows papers. actual story policymakers saw FOMC meeting closer first-release values.
code: inflation_now <- fred_series(   c("CPIAUCSL", "CPILFESL"),   from = "2018-01-01",   transform = "yoy_pct",   format = "wide" ) plot(inflation_now, ylab = "% YoY",      main = "Headline vs core CPI, latest vintage")

title: Tracking core inflation revisions
text: Use fred_first_release() get value published release date, subsequent revisions: two lines diverge meaningfully across pivot points (early 2022, late 2023). divergence data revision.
code: core_first <- fred_first_release(   "CPILFESL",   from = "2018-01-01",   units = "pc1"   # YoY percent change ) core_latest <- fred_series(   "CPILFESL",   from = "2018-01-01",   transform = "yoy_pct" )  # Plot both on one chart plot(core_first$date, core_first$value, type = "l",      xlab = "", ylab = "% YoY",      main = "Core CPI: first release vs latest vintage",      ylim = range(c(core_first$value, core_latest$value), na.rm = TRUE)) graphics::lines(core_latest$date, core_latest$value, col = "tomato") graphics::legend("topleft",                  legend = c("First release", "Latest vintage"),                  col = c("black", "tomato"), lty = 1, bty = "n")

title: Tracking core inflation revisions
text: Pull panel core CPI seen FOMC SEP meeting 2024. data informed Summary Economic Projections forecasts. realtime_start column tells vintage row belongs . visualise four lines: vintage panel makes visually obvious revisions accumulated.
code: sep_meetings <- fred_fomc_dates(year = 2024, sep_only = TRUE) sep_meetings panel <- fred_real_time_panel(   "CPILFESL",   vintages = sep_meetings$date,   from = "2022-01-01" ) head(panel) panel_wide <- stats::reshape(   panel[, c("date", "value", "realtime_start")],   idvar = "date", timevar = "realtime_start",   direction = "wide" ) names(panel_wide) <- sub("^value\\.", "v", names(panel_wide)) panel_wide <- panel_wide[order(panel_wide$date), ]  cols <- c("#1F77B4", "#FF7F0E", "#2CA02C", "#D62728") plot(panel_wide$date, panel_wide[[2]], type = "l", col = cols[1],      ylim = range(unlist(panel_wide[, -1]), na.rm = TRUE),      xlab = "", ylab = "Core CPI level",      main = "Core CPI as seen at four 2024 SEP meetings") for (i in 3:ncol(panel_wide)) {   graphics::lines(panel_wide$date, panel_wide[[i]], col = cols[i - 1L]) } graphics::legend("topleft",                  legend = format(sep_meetings$date, "%b %Y"),                  col = cols, lty = 1, bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

title: Tracking core inflation revisions
text: research-grade summary, use fred_vintage_revisions(): output gives, per observation date: many vintages exist, first final value, total revision (level percent), mean SD inter-vintage revisions, elapsed days first publication final. Use : Pick low-revision series nowcasting (small revision_sd). Identify observation periods revisions unusually large (typically structural-break seasonal-factor years).
code: rev <- fred_vintage_revisions("CPILFESL", from = "2020-01-01") head(rev) summary(rev$revision_total_pct)

title: Real-time GDP nowcasting with monthly indicators
code: library(fred) library(nowcast)

title: Real-time GDP nowcasting with monthly indicators
text: Start small, defensible set monthly indicators released early month previous month. Industrial production, retail sales, employment, ISM-style activity, financial conditions standard choices. GDP , transformed quarter--quarter annualised growth (FRED’s pca transform = "annualised"):
code: indicators <- c(   "INDPRO",   # Industrial production (15th)   "RSAFS",    # Retail sales advance (mid-month)   "PAYEMS",   # Nonfarm payrolls (first Friday)   "NFCI",     # Chicago Fed financial conditions (weekly)   "UMCSENT"   # Michigan consumer sentiment (mid + late month) )  monthly <- fred_series(indicators, from = "2000-01-01", format = "wide") head(monthly) gdp_growth <- fred_series(   "GDPC1",   from = "2000-01-01",   transform = "annualised" ) plot(gdp_growth, main = "Real GDP, QoQ annualised growth")

title: Real-time GDP nowcasting with monthly indicators
text: naive approach: regress GDP growth aggregated monthly indicators using latest data. ad-hoc nowcasts use. estimate bridge equation nowcast, inputs need single data frame target series indicators side side. sketch illustrative; see ?nowcast::nc_bridge actual formula interface. fit looks impressive using revised indicator values available real time.
code: # Aggregate monthlies to quarterly using fred_aggregate() monthly_long <- fred_series(indicators, from = "2000-01-01") quarterly <- fred_aggregate(monthly_long, fun = "mean", by = "quarter") head(quarterly) # Pseudo-code: build a wide quarterly frame for nc_bridge(formula, data) panel <- merge(   fred_series("GDPC1", from = "2000-01-01", transform = "annualised"),   fred_aggregate(     fred_series(indicators, from = "2000-01-01", format = "wide"),     fun = "mean", by = "quarter"   ),   by = "date" ) bridge <- nowcast::nc_bridge(value ~ INDPRO + RSAFS + PAYEMS, data = panel) summary(bridge)

title: Real-time GDP nowcasting with monthly indicators
text: Replace indicator series first-release vintage rerun. discipline: forecast date, fetch published date. can now run bridge equation per forecast date compare real-time forecast eventual final-vintage GDP value. nowcast package helpers ; see nowcast::nc_backtest().
code: forecast_dates <- seq(as.Date("2015-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-01"),                       by = "quarter")  backtest <- lapply(forecast_dates, function(d) {   ind_rt <- fred_real_time_panel(indicators, vintages = d, from = "2000-01-01")   gdp_rt <- fred_as_of("GDPC1", date = d, from = "2000-01-01",                        units = "pca")   list(date = d, ind = ind_rt, gdp = gdp_rt) })

title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: fred provides clean, tidy access economic data Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) API directly R.

title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) database maintained Federal Reserve Bank St. Louis - one twelve regional Reserve Banks US Federal Reserve System. Fed’s Board Governors Washington sets monetary policy, regional banks developed research specialisms. St. Louis carved reputation applied economic research data dissemination, FRED launched 1991 dial-bulletin board moving web mid-1990s. since grown arguably single important free data platform macroeconomic research, now hosting 800,000 time series covering GDP, employment, inflation, interest rates, trade, financial markets, . makes FRED unusual role aggregator. database doesn’t just publish St. Louis Fed’s data - pulls series 118 different sources, including Bureau Labor Statistics, Bureau Economic Analysis, Census Bureau, Treasury Department, World Bank, dozens US international agencies. Rather visiting agency’s website individually navigating different data formats, FRED gives single, consistent interface . ’s closest thing economics universal data catalogue.

title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: FRED provides free REST API gives programmatic access full database. API supports searching series, fetching observations, browsing category tree, tracking data releases revisions, applying server-side transformations like percent change frequency aggregation - without needing visit website. Anyone can register free API key. package wraps FRED API can pull data directly R single function call. Rather constructing API URLs hand, fred_series("GDP") fetches data, parses response, returns tidy data frame. can fetch multiple series , apply transformations, search keyword, browse categories, track revisions - R. Results cached locally repeated calls instant.

what: How is this different from fredr?
title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: existing R package called fredr, written Sam Boysel Davis Vaughan published CRAN 2021. ’s solid package, hasn’t updated since August 2021. ’s better users: Multiple series one call - fredr can fetch one series time, pulling GDP, unemployment, CPI means three separate requests manually binding results. fred, fred_series(c("GDP", "UNRATE", "CPIAUCSL")) returns everything one tidy data frame. Local caching - fredr downloads data fresh every time run script. ’re knitting Quarto report tweaking chart title, re-downloads everything scratch every render. fred caches results locally first download, subsequent calls instant don’t touch API. Automatic pagination - FRED’s API returns results pages 1,000. category 2,500 series, fredr gives first 1,000 stops - users won’t realise ’re getting incomplete data. fred automatically fetches pages stitches together. ’s better hood: Modern HTTP stack - fredr depends httr, superseded httr2. fred uses httr2 built-rate-limit retries graceful error handling API unreachable. Fewer dependencies - fred depends 3 third-party packages (cli, httr2, tools) plus base R. fredr pulls httr, jsonlite, rlang, tibble, purrr. Research-grade infrastructure - offline catalogue ~50 popular series, NBER recession reference dates, FOMC meeting reference dates, vintage revision summaries, BibTeX/YAML reproducibility helpers, default plot() method NBER recession shading. Actively maintained - fredr updates since August 2021.

what: Step 3: Save the key so R can find it
title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: best approach store key file called .Renviron, R reads automatically every time starts. means set never think . Option : RStudio Open RStudio console, type file.edit("~/.Renviron") press Enter - opens (creates) file Add line, replacing placeholder actual key: Save file close Restart R (Session → Restart R, press Ctrl+Shift+F10 / Cmd+Shift+F10) Option B: terminal Open Terminal (Mac/Linux) Command Prompt (Windows) Run command, replacing placeholder actual key: Restart R Option C: Set current session just want try things without editing files, can set key temporarily: lasts close R - ’ll need run next session.
code: FRED_API_KEY=abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890 echo 'FRED_API_KEY=abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890' >> ~/.Renviron library(fred) fred_set_key("abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890")

what: How do I find the series I need?
title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: Every dataset FRED short series ID. example, GDP US quarterly GDP, UNRATE US unemployment rate, CPIAUCSL Consumer Price Index. need know series ID pull data. four ways find : 1. Curated offline catalogue using fred_catalogue(). Around 50 -used series, grouped category, API call needed: 2. Search R using fred_search() full FRED database (live API): 3. Browse category tree using fred_browse() (top-level categories offline; deeper levels hit API): 4. Browse FRED website fred.stlouisfed.org. series ID, pass fred_series().
code: fred_catalogue(category = "Inflation") #> # FRED: catalogue · 6 rows #>          id                                                title  frequency  category #>   CPIAUCSL                CPI for All Urban Consumers: All Items          M Inflation #>   CPILFESL  CPI for All Urban Consumers: Less Food and Energy            M Inflation #>      PCEPI         PCE: Chain-type Price Index                            M Inflation #>      ...  fred_catalogue(query = "mortgage") #> # FRED: catalogue · 1 row #>           id                                title frequency       category #>  MORTGAGE30US  30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage Average         W Interest Rates fred_search("consumer price index") fred_browse() #> FRED top-level categories #> ------------------------- #>   32991  Money, Banking, & Finance #>      10  Population, Employment, & Labor Markets #>   32992  National Accounts #>       1  Production & Business Activity #>   32455  Prices #>      ...

text: multi-series analysis ’s often easier one column per series. Pass format = "wide": header top one-line summary query (series count, observations, transform, frequency, vintage). Downstream code can treat result normal data frame.
code: macro <- fred_series(c("GDP", "UNRATE", "CPIAUCSL"),                      from = "2020-01-01", format = "wide") head(macro) #> # FRED: 3 series · 60 obs · format=wide #>         date       GDP UNRATE CPIAUCSL #>   2020-01-01  21561.14    3.5  259.037 #>   2020-02-01        NA    3.5  259.250 #>   2020-03-01  21289.27    4.4  258.115 #>   ...

title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: raw FRED units codes (pch, pc1, cca, etc.) work fine, ’re always memorable. can pass transform = instead: Available aliases: level, raw, diff, change, yoy_diff, qoq_pct, mom_pct, pop_pct, yoy_pct, annualised, qoq_annualised, log, log_diff, log_diff_annualised. units transform arguments mutually exclusive.
code: # Year-on-year percent change in CPI fred_series("CPIAUCSL", transform = "yoy_pct")  # Continuously compounded annual rate fred_series("GDP", transform = "log_diff_annualised")  # Quarter-on-quarter level change fred_series("GDP", transform = "diff")

title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
text: FRED keeps full revision history series via sister database, ALFRED. fred exposes four helpers, add realtime_start realtime_end columns returned data frame. building blocks pseudo-real-time forecasting backtests revision studies. cache separately default (latest-vintage) cache, calling fred_series("GDP") fred_as_of("GDP", "2020-03-01") keep distinct cache entries.
code: # 1. The series as it appeared on a specific date gdp_then <- fred_as_of("GDP", date = "2020-03-01")  # 2. Only the first release of each observation, never revised gdp_first <- fred_first_release("GDP", from = "2010-01-01")  # 3. Every vintage in the revision history #    (potentially large - narrow with from/to for long series) gdp_all <- fred_all_vintages("GDP", from = "2020-01-01")  # 4. A panel of selected vintage snapshots gdp_panel <- fred_real_time_panel(   "GDP",   vintages = c("2020-04-30", "2020-07-31", "2020-10-31", "2021-01-31") )

code: # Aggregate daily to monthly with any summary yields_m <- fred_aggregate(fred_series("DGS10", from = "2023-01-01"),                            fun = "mean", by = "month")  # Fill NAs with last-observation-carry-forward or linear interpolation filled <- fred_interpolate(fred_series("GDPC1"), method = "linear")  # Extract data inside a window around event dates (e.g. FOMC meetings) ur <- fred_series("UNRATE", from = "2023-01-01") sep <- fred_fomc_dates(year = 2024, sep_only = TRUE) fred_event_window(ur, events = sep$date, window = c(-60L, 60L))

what: Reproducibility: cite series and snapshot manifests
title: Access Federal Reserve Economic Data
code: # BibTeX citation pinned to a vintage date fred_cite_series("GDPC1", vintage_date = "2024-12-18", format = "bibtex")  # Plain text citation fred_cite_series("UNRATE", format = "text")  # YAML manifest with MD5 hash per object - save alongside paper code gdp <- fred_series("GDPC1", from = "2020-01-01") ur  <- fred_series("UNRATE", from = "2020-01-01") m <- fred_manifest(gdp = gdp, unrate = ur,                    file = "data/manifest.yml")  # Per-observation revision statistics fred_vintage_revisions("GDPC1", from = "2018-01-01")

title: fred: Access 'Federal Reserve Economic Data' — fred-package
text: Provides clean, tidy access economic data 'Federal Reserve Economic Data' ('FRED') API https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/fred/. 'FRED' maintained 'Federal Reserve Bank St. Louis' contains 800,000 time series 118 sources covering GDP, employment, inflation, interest rates, trade, . Dedicated functions fetch series observations, search series, browse categories, releases, tags, retrieve series metadata. Multiple series can fetched single call, long wide format. Server-side unit transformations (percent change, log, etc.) frequency aggregation supported, readable transform aliases 'yoy_pct' 'log_diff'. Real-time vintage helpers (built 'ALFRED') return series appeared given date, first-release version, every revision, panel selected vintages. offline curated catalogue around fifty popular series, NBER recession reference dates, FOMC meeting dates support discoverability event-study workflows. Default 'plot' method shades NBER recession periods. Reproducibility helpers produce BibTeX plain-text citations YAML manifests per-object hashes. Data cached locally subsequent calls. product uses 'FRED' API endorsed certified 'Federal Reserve Bank St. Louis'.

title: fred: Access 'Federal Reserve Economic Data' — fred-package

what: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
text: Aggregates long-format fred_tbl (date, series_id, value) wide-format fred_tbl (date plus one column per series) coarser calendar frequency. long format, aggregation performed per series_id; wide format, per numeric column.

title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
code: fred_aggregate(data, fun = "mean", by = "month")

title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
text: data fred_tbl data.frame date column. fun Character. Aggregation function. One "mean", "sum", "first", "last", "median", "min", "max". Default "mean". Character. Target frequency. One "week", "month", "quarter", "year". Default "month".

title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
text: fred_tbl columns input, date collapsed period start.

title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
text: Use , say, daily Treasury yields need monthly average, weekly initial claims aggregated monthly totals. server-side aggregation mirrors FRED's interpolation conventions, pass frequency = "m" fred_series() instead.

title: Aggregate FRED observations to a coarser frequency — fred_aggregate
code: # Synthetic example: aggregate daily synthetic data to monthly means d <- seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-06-30"), by = "day") daily <- data.frame(date = d, series_id = "X", value = rnorm(length(d))) fred_aggregate(daily, fun = "mean", by = "month") #> # FRED: 6 rows #>         date series_id       value #> 1 2024-01-01         X -0.20970417 #> 2 2024-02-01         X  0.10408963 #> 3 2024-03-01         X  0.15213658 #> 4 2024-04-01         X  0.11632121 #> 5 2024-05-01         X  0.13332977 #> 6 2024-06-01         X -0.06317285  # Wide-format input also works wide <- data.frame(date = d, A = rnorm(length(d)), B = rnorm(length(d))) fred_aggregate(wide, fun = "sum", by = "quarter") #> # FRED: 2 rows #>         date          A         B #> 1 2024-01-01 -0.8722382 -8.711310 #> 2 2024-04-01 25.8291942 -3.791956  # \donttest{ op <- options(fred.cache_dir = tempdir()) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   daily_yields <- fred_series("DGS10", from = "2023-01-01")   monthly_yields <- fred_aggregate(daily_yields, fun = "mean", by = "month") } # } options(op) # }

title: Fetch every vintage of a series — fred_all_vintages
text: Returns full revision history: one row per (observation date, realtime range) combination. FRED API's output_type = 2 mode. result can reshaped vintage matrix used compute revision statistics.

text: series_id Character. One FRED series IDs. , Optional observation date range. units Character. Raw FRED units code. Default "lin". frequency, aggregation Optional frequency aggregation arguments (see fred_series()). cache Logical. Cache results locally. Default TRUE.

title: Fetch every vintage of a series — fred_all_vintages
text: fred_tbl columns date, series_id, value, realtime_start, realtime_end. realtime_start realtime_end columns identify vintage window row.

what: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
title: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
text: Produces citation string single FRED series BibTeX, plain text, bibentry form. Works offline default: citation falls back series ID title. Pass fetch_metadata = TRUE call fred_info() use official series title (requires API key).

title: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
code: fred_cite_series(   series_id,   vintage_date = NULL,   format = c("bibtex", "text", "bibentry"),   fetch_metadata = FALSE )

title: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
text: series_id Character. single FRED series ID. vintage_date Optional character Date. Vintage date data accessed . Defaults today's date ("accessed" date). format Character. One "bibtex" (default), "text", "bibentry" (R utils::bibentry object). fetch_metadata Logical. TRUE, call fred_info() fetch official series title. Requires API key. Default FALSE.

title: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
text: character string ("bibtex", "text") bibentry object.

title: Generate a citation for a FRED series — fred_cite_series
text: Cite specific vintage passing vintage_date. essential reproducible research: 2023 GDP figure published October 2023 may differ materially observation appears today.

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
text: Given fred_tbl (data frame date column) vector event dates, returns one row per (event, observation) pair observation falls inside requested window. Useful event studies around FOMC decisions, recession peaks, release dates.

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
code: fred_event_window(data, events, window = c(-30L, 90L))

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
text: data fred_tbl data.frame date column. events character Date vector event dates. window Integer length-2. Days (negative zero) (positive zero) event date. Default c(-30L, 90L).

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
text: fred_tbl original columns plus event_date days_from_event.

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
text: Long-format input series_id/value supported, wide-format input fred_series(..., format = "wide"). window calendar days; monthly quarterly data, choose window large enough capture least one observation.

title: Extract data windows around event dates — fred_event_window
code: # Synthetic example — works offline d <- seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-12-31"), by = "month") df <- data.frame(date = d, value = seq_along(d)) events <- as.Date(c("2024-03-15", "2024-09-15")) fred_event_window(df, events = events, window = c(-30L, 60L)) #> # FRED: 6 rows #>         date value event_date days_from_event #> 1 2024-03-01     3 2024-03-15             -14 #> 2 2024-04-01     4 2024-03-15              17 #> 3 2024-05-01     5 2024-03-15              47 #> 4 2024-09-01     9 2024-09-15             -14 #> 5 2024-10-01    10 2024-09-15              16 #> 6 2024-11-01    11 2024-09-15              47  # \donttest{ op <- options(fred.cache_dir = tempdir()) # With live FRED data: UNRATE around 2024 SEP meetings (needs API key) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   ur <- fred_series("UNRATE", from = "2023-01-01")   sep <- fred_fomc_dates(year = 2024, sep_only = TRUE)   fred_event_window(ur, events = sep$date, window = c(-60L, 60L)) } # } options(op) # }

what: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
text: Fills NA values value column (long format) numeric columns (wide format). Two methods supported: last-observation-carry-forward ("locf") linear interpolation adjacent observed values ("linear"). Use mixed-frequency analysis low-frequency series needs interpolated higher frequency.

title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
code: fred_interpolate(data, method = c("locf", "linear"))

title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
text: data fred_tbl data.frame date column. method Character. "locf" (default) carries last observed value forward. "linear" linear interpolation adjacent non-NA values.

title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
text: fred_tbl interior NAs filled (see boundary note ).

title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
text: Boundary behaviour: method = "locf", leading NAs remain NA prior observation carry forward. method = "linear", neither leading trailing NAs filled stats::approx() called rule = 1 (extrapolation). need extrapolation, post-process result.

title: Fill missing values in a FRED series — fred_interpolate
code: # Synthetic example: fill interior NAs d <- seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 6L) df <- data.frame(date = d, series_id = "X",                  value = c(NA, 2, NA, NA, 5, NA)) fred_interpolate(df, method = "locf") #> # FRED: 6 rows #>         date series_id value #> 1 2024-01-01         X    NA #> 2 2024-02-01         X     2 #> 3 2024-03-01         X     2 #> 4 2024-04-01         X     2 #> 5 2024-05-01         X     5 #> 6 2024-06-01         X     5 fred_interpolate(df, method = "linear") #> # FRED: 6 rows #>         date series_id value #> 1 2024-01-01         X    NA #> 2 2024-02-01         X     2 #> 3 2024-03-01         X     3 #> 4 2024-04-01         X     4 #> 5 2024-05-01         X     5 #> 6 2024-06-01         X    NA  # \donttest{ op <- options(fred.cache_dir = tempdir()) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   gdp <- fred_series("GDPC1", from = "2020-01-01")   gdp_monthly <- fred_interpolate(gdp, method = "linear") } # } options(op) # }

what: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
text: main function package. Downloads time series observations FRED returns tidy data frame. Multiple series can fetched single call, either long wide format.

title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
code: fred_series(   series_id,   from = NULL,   to = NULL,   units = "lin",   transform = NULL,   frequency = NULL,   aggregation = "avg",   format = c("long", "wide"),   cache = TRUE )

title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
text: series_id Character. One FRED series IDs (e.g. "GDP", c("GDP", "UNRATE", "CPIAUCSL")). Optional start date. Character ("YYYY-MM-DD") Date. Optional end date. Character ("YYYY-MM-DD") Date. units Character. Raw FRED units code. Default "lin" (levels). Mutually exclusive transform. transform Character. Readable transformation name. See Details. frequency Character. Frequency aggregation. One "d" (daily), "w" (weekly), "bw" (biweekly), "m" (monthly), "q" (quarterly), "sa" (semiannual), "" (annual), NULL (native frequency, default). aggregation Character. Aggregation method frequency specified. One "avg" (default), "sum", "eop" (end period). format Character. "long" (default) returns one row per (series_id, date). "wide" returns one row per date one column per series. cache Logical. TRUE (default), results cached locally returned cache subsequent calls. Set FALSE force fresh download API.

title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
text: fred_tbl (data.frame subclass prints one-line provenance header). long format, columns date, series_id, value. wide format, columns date plus one numeric column per series.

title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series
text: FRED supports server-side unit transformations via units argument. avoids need compute growth rates log transforms locally. readability can pass transform instead units: "level", "raw" -levels (default) "diff", "change" -change previous period "yoy_diff" -change one year ago "qoq_pct", "mom_pct", "pop_pct" -percent change previous period "yoy_pct" -percent change one year ago "annualised", "qoq_annualised" -compounded annual rate change "log" -natural log "log_diff" -continuously compounded rate change "log_diff_annualised" -continuously compounded annual rate Raw FRED units codes ("lin", "chg", "ch1", "pch", "pc1", "pca", "cch", "cca", "log") also accepted.

title: Fetch observations for one or more FRED series — fred_series

title: fred 0.3.0
text: research-grade upgrade three logical blocks: (1) discoverability reference data, (2) workflow utilities default plot method, (3) reproducibility helpers. 35 exports + 4 S3 methods, 275 tests, three new vignettes.

title: fred 0.3.0
text: New fred_catalogue() returns offline curated catalogue around 50 widely used FRED series (GDP, employment, inflation, rates, housing, financial, money/credit, trade/FX, consumer, fiscal). Filter category free-text. API call required. New fred_browse() pretty-prints FRED category tree. arguments, shows eight top-level categories static reference (API call). Pass category_id drill children. New fred_recession_dates() returns NBER business-cycle reference dates (peak, trough, duration) since 1857. Pass vector dates flag get back per-observation -recession indicators use regression covariate. New fred_fomc_dates() returns FOMC scheduled meeting decision dates 2017 2025, including SEP-meeting flags. Selected unscheduled meetings stress periods included. fred_tbl now threads fred_search(), fred_category(), fred_category_children(), fred_category_series(), fred_releases(), fred_release_series(), fred_release_dates(), fred_sources(), fred_source_releases(), fred_tags(), fred_related_tags(), fred_updates(). print headers now show endpoint search query (applicable). New summary.fred_tbl() prints query metadata, dimensions, date range, value range standard summary.data.frame output. New [.fred_tbl() preserves fred_tbl class fred_query attribute subsetting.

title: fred 0.3.0
text: New fred_event_window() extracts data inside c(, ) day window around event dates. Works long wide format. Handy event studies around FOMC decisions, recession peaks, release dates. New fred_aggregate() aggregates long wide format data coarser calendar frequency (week / month / quarter / year) using mean, sum, first, last, median, min, max. Complements server-side aggregation fred_series(frequency = ...). New fred_interpolate() fills NA values via last-observation-carry- forward ("locf") linear interpolation ("linear"). Useful mixed-frequency analysis. New plot.fred_tbl() default plot method. Detects long wide format, draws one line per series, shades NBER recession periods. Uses base graphics: ggplot2 dependency.

title: fred 0.3.0
text: New fred_cite_series() produces citation FRED series BibTeX, plain text, bibentry form. Works offline (falls back series ID title); pass fetch_metadata = TRUE use official series title fred_info(). Supports vintage-date pinning cited data reproducible even revisions. New fred_manifest() snapshots one fred_tbl objects YAML manifest query metadata, dimensions, date range, MD5 hash object. Saving manifest alongside paper code lets reviewers verify underlying data unchanged. New fred_vintage_revisions() returns per-observation revision summary statistics (n_vintages, first/final value, total revision, mean/SD inter-vintage changes, days final). Useful choosing low-revision series nowcasting real-time analysis.

title: fred 0.3.0
text: New vignette multi-series-workflows: fetch, transform, widen, plot. New vignette nowcasting--fred: pseudo-real-time GDP nowcasting using monthly indicators, vintage-aware backtesting (pairs nowcast package). New vignette inflation-revisions: tracking core inflation revisions using fred_real_time_panel() aligned FOMC SEP meeting dates.

title: fred 0.3.0
text: CITATION updated v0.3.0 (outdated 0.1.0). New CITATION.cff file repository root GitHub citation widget Zenodo deposit workflow.

title: fred 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-11

title: fred 0.2.0
text: fred_series() gains format argument. Pass format = "wide" get one row per date column per series, instead default long layout. fred_series() gains transform argument readable aliases FRED units codes: "level", "diff", "yoy_diff", "qoq_pct", "yoy_pct", "annualised", "log", "log_diff", . raw units codes still work; transform units mutually exclusive. fred_as_of() returns series appeared chosen vintage date. fred_first_release() returns initial release observation, subsequent revisions. fred_all_vintages() returns full revision history. fred_real_time_panel() returns values available chosen set vintage dates. New fred_cache_info() reports cache directory, file count, total size, per-file metadata. Useful debugging stale results. observation results now returned fred_tbl, thin data.frame subclass one-line provenance header showing query (series count, observation count, units, transform, frequency, vintage). header informational; downstream code can keep treating result plain data frame.

title: fred 0.2.0
text: cache key format unchanged default arguments, caches built 0.1.x still picked upgrade. Realtime, output-type, vintage requests cached distinct keys.
